one_line_takeaway: The first systematic survey of adversarial attacks and defenses on text-to-image diffusion models, contributing taxonomies, a metric/dataset catalog, and a clear diagnosis that current attacks lack imperceptibility while most defenses fail against malicious-word-omitting adversarial prompts.
key_finding: Two structural gaps dominate the field per the survey. (1) On attacks: targeted (safety-bypassing) attacks outnumber untargeted (robustness) attacks, and most attacks use word- or sentence-level perturbations that add conspicuous noise and are therefore easily detected (poor imperceptibility); most also produce grammatically incorrect prompts flaggable by a grammar detector. (2) On defenses: most safety defenses (and nearly all model-editing methods in Table 2) address only explicit MALICIOUS prompts and remain vulnerable to ADVERSARIAL prompts that omit malicious words; only a handful (e.g., RACE, Receler, AdvUnlearn among editors; Latent Guard, GuardT2I among external safeguards) consider adversarial prompts, and even those cover only a subset of adversarial-prompt types. Robustness defense against grammatically-incorrect subtly-noised prompts is essentially absent, which the authors flag as an open problem.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not a method paper): it is presented as the first review dedicated to adversarial attacks and defenses on text-to-image diffusion models (AD-on-T2IDM). The authors' own contribution is the organizing scaffolding, not any attack/defense technique (all named methods such as ATM, Zhuang et al., SneakyPrompt, MMA, ESD, UCE, Latent Guard, GuardT2I, etc. are CITED prior work being reviewed, not the authors' own systems). Specifically the paper contributes: (1) a three-axis taxonomy of attacks (Target: untargeted vs targeted; Knowledge: white-box vs black-box; Perturbation granularity: character/word/sentence-level), further split for targeted attacks by which safeguard they bypass (external, internal, black-box); (2) a parallel taxonomy of defenses (robustness vs safety; and for safety, external safeguards = prompt classifier / prompt transformation, vs internal safeguards = model editing / inference guidance), including a comparison table (Table 2) of ~17 model-editing methods; (3) a consolidated catalog of evaluation metrics and datasets used across the field; and (4) an analysis of open challenges (attack imperceptibility, defense coverage of adversarial prompts). A curated GitHub repository (Awesome-AD-on-T2IDM) accompanies it.
threat_model: As synthesized by the survey, the adversary perturbs the input text prompt to a text-to-image diffusion model (chiefly open-source Stable Diffusion; some black-box work targets DALL-E 2/3 and Midjourney). Two adversary intents are distinguished. Untargeted attacks add subtle prompt noise to break ROBUSTNESS, causing the output image's semantics to deviate from the clean prompt (e.g., inserting five random characters, or typos/glyph/phonetic edits). Targeted attacks aim to defeat SAFETY safeguards: they craft an adversarial prompt that omits explicit malicious words yet still induces the model to generate malicious content (sexual, violent/bloody, hateful, copyright-infringing, or politically sensitive images), bypassing external filters (blacklists, prompt/image classifiers), internal safeguards (concept-erasure model editing, inference guidance), or black-box commercial safeguards. Adversary knowledge ranges from white-box (full weights/architecture, gradient/PGD optimization) to black-box (API query access, Bayesian optimization, reinforcement learning, genetic search, or LLM-driven prompt rewriting).
limitations: As a survey its value is coverage/organization, not new evidence: it reports no head-to-head empirical benchmark, so relative attack/defense strength is described qualitatively rather than measured. It deliberately omits deep coverage of robustness-improvement / controllable-generation methods (deferring to other surveys). Scope is bounded by the June 1, 2024 arXiv cutoff, so it predates later 2024+ attacks/defenses. Coverage centers on open-source Stable Diffusion, with lighter treatment of closed commercial models. The taxonomy leans on prior NLP/CV adversarial-survey axes, and some categorizations (e.g., which optimization space is 'hardest') are argued by reasoning about computational complexity rather than measurement.

datasets_used: Non-malicious clean datasets cataloged: ImageNet (1,000 categories), MSCOCO (82,783 training / 40,504 testing images, 5 captions each), LAION-COCO (600 million image-text pairs, subset of LAION-5B), DiffusionDB (14 million Stable-Diffusion-generated images)

Malicious datasets cataloged: Unsafe Diffusion (30 manually crafted sexual/bloody/political prompts), SneakyPrompt (200 ChatGPT-generated sexual/bloody prompts), I2P (4,703 inappropriate real-user prompts across hate/harassment/violence/self-harm/nudity/shocking/illegal), MMA (1,000 NSFW prompts sampled from LAION-COCO), MACE dataset (200 celebrities), Image Synthesis Style Studies Database (thousands of artist styles)

Adversarial datasets cataloged: Adversarial Nibbler (3,412 manually crafted safeguard-bypassing prompts), MMA (1,000 generated adversarial prompts), Zhang et al. (10 target objects x 500 adversarial prompts = 5,000)
quantitative_results: Motivation stats (cited): Stable Diffusion >10 million users (some sources cite >12 billion images generated, ~80% of AI images online); Midjourney ~14.5 million users

Table 2 tabulates ~17 model-editing defenses; only 3 (RACE, Receler, AdvUnlearn) are marked as providing safety against BOTH malicious (M) and adversarial (A) prompts, the rest only against malicious prompts
Among external/internal safety defenses in Fig. 7, only 5 methods (Latent Guard, GuardT2I, RACE, Receler, AdvUnlearn) consider adversarial prompts in addition to malicious prompts

Untargeted attacks reviewed in depth: 3 (ATM and Zhuang et al. white-box; Gao et al. black-box, character-level); targeted attacks span external-safeguard (~7), internal-safeguard (5), and black-box-safeguard (multiple) methods
MACE dataset celebrities are recognized with >99% accuracy by the GIPHY Celebrity Detector (a dataset-construction quality figure, not a survey result)
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the experimental sense: as a survey the paper runs no benchmark and reports no win/loss numbers of its own. It instead provides structured qualitative comparisons of prior methods (e.g., Table 2 compares model-editing methods by optimization space, edited parameters, optimization method, and malicious/adversarial safety coverage; targeted attacks compared by attack objective, perturbation, optimization, and additional factors).
